title: How Crypto Signals Work
description: Learn how professional signal ideas are generated, structured, and delivered to traders.
content:
The Fundamentals of Crypto Signals

In the fast-moving world of digital assets, a 'crypto signal' is essentially a trade idea or recommendation delivered to a group of traders. However, a professional signal is much more than just a ticker name and a 'buy' button.

The Anatomy of a Professional Signal

At Yaga Calls, every signal we share follows a strict, repeatable structure designed for clarity and risk management:

  • Asset Pair: Which coin we are trading (e.g., BTC/USDT or SOL/USDT).
  • Direction: Whether it is a 'Long' (buy) or 'Short' (sell) setup.
  • Entry Zone: A specific price range where the setup is valid. We rarely use a single fixed price.
  • Take-Profit (TP) Targets: Predetermined levels where we plan to take profit, usually in tiers.
  • Stop-Loss (SL): The most important level—where the trade thesis is invalidated and we exit to protect capital.

How Signals are Generated

We don't guess. Our signals are the result of three layers of analysis: Technical Structure (chart patterns), On-Chain Data (whale movements), and Market Narratives (the 'story' driving the price). Only when these three align do we share a setup on Telegram.

'A signal without a stop-loss is not a trade—it is a gamble.'

title: Free vs Paid Crypto Signals
description: Understanding the difference between public teaser groups and premium research-driven setups.
content:
Understanding the Difference

Telegram is filled with thousands of crypto signal groups. Understanding the difference between 'Free' and 'Paid' (Premium) services is critical for your survival as a trader.

Free Crypto Signal Groups

Most free groups serve as educational teasers. At Yaga Calls, our free Telegram group provides:

  • General market updates and macro analysis.
  • Occasional 'public' setups for transparency.
  • Proof of recent premium successes.

The primary limitation of free groups is the lack of depth. You get the 'what' but rarely the 'why' or the ongoing management of the trade.

Premium Crypto Signals (The Yaga Advantage)

Premium access is designed for traders who want a professional edge. The benefits include:

  • High-Conviction Setups: Filtered opportunities that meet strict criteria.
  • Direct Guidance: Manual support and updates as the trade evolves.
  • Risk Context: Deeper explanations of why a specific stop-loss or entry was chosen.

Is it worth it? If the cost of the subscription is significantly lower than the value of the time saved and the mistakes avoided, the answer is yes. However, it is never a guarantee of profit.

title: How to Read Entry, Target, and Stop Loss
description: Mastering the three pillars of trade management before you place your next order.
content:
Mastering the Execution

Getting a signal is only 10% of the battle. The other 90% is executing it correctly without letting emotion interfere with the math.

1. The Entry Zone

Never 'chase' a signal. If the price has already moved 5% past our suggested entry zone, the risk/reward ratio has shifted against you. Wait for a retest or move on to the next setup.

2. Tiered Take-Profits

We believe in 'paying ourselves' along the way. We typically provide 3-4 TP targets. When TP1 is hit, we often move our Stop-Loss to the entry price (Break-Even) to eliminate risk from the trade.

3. The Invalidation Point (Stop-Loss)

A stop-loss is a mathematical necessity. It is the price at which our narrative or technical thesis is proven wrong. Never move your stop-loss further away hoping for a bounce. This is how small losses turn into account-ending disasters.

Pro Tip

Use 'Limit Orders' for your entries and 'Stop-Market' orders for your protection to ensure you aren't watching the charts 24/7.

title: How to Choose a Crypto Signals Provider
description: The essential checklist for identifying trustworthy traders in a sea of Telegram noise.
content:
Identifying Trust in a Sea of Scams

Choosing a signal provider is an investment in itself. Here is the checklist we recommend every trader uses:

1. Transparency of Results

Does the provider show their losses as well as their wins? Be wary of '100% Win Rate' groups. They don't exist. Look for a track record that includes 'Selected Examples' with full context.

2. Focus on Risk Management

Does the group talk more about 'lambos' or 'stop-losses'? A professional group like Yaga Calls prioritizes capital preservation over hype.

3. Methodology and Logic

Do they explain *why* they are taking a trade? If a signal is just a ticker and a 'buy' command, you aren't learning anything. You want a provider that educates you through their analysis.

4. Manual Support

In a manual onboarding system like ours, you have the ability to ask questions and confirm availability. This human element is often missing in 'bot-only' groups.

title: How to Avoid Pump Signal Scams
description: Learn the common red flags of 'pump and dump' groups and how to protect your capital.
content:
Recognizing the Red Flags

The 'Pump and Dump' is the oldest scam in crypto. A group owner buys a low-liquidity coin, then 'signals' it to thousands of people to create a price spike so they can sell into your buy orders.

Common Scam Traits:

  • Promised Returns: Phrases like 'Guaranteed 500% profit tonight!' are 100% scams.
  • Urgency: 'Buy now before it's too late!'—professional traders don't use FOMO.
  • No Liquidity: Signals for coins you've never heard of on obscure exchanges.

At Yaga Calls, we focus on liquid assets and established market narratives. We don't participate in 'scheduled pumps' because they are designed to make the group owner rich at your expense.

title: Risk Management for Crypto Signals
description: The most important lesson in trading: how to size your positions and protect your portfolio equity.
content:
Trading by the Numbers

Risk management is the only thing that separates a trader from a gambler. Even with a 40% win rate, a trader with good risk management can be profitable. A trader with a 90% win rate and zero risk management can go broke in a single trade.

The 1-2% Position Sizing Rule

At Yaga Calls, we strictly enforce a 1-2% risk per trade rule. This means that if your stop-loss is hit, you only lose 1% or 2% of your total account balance—not 1% of your position size.

Example Scenario:

  • Balance: $10,000
  • Risk (1%): $100 per trade
  • Stop Loss Distance: 10%
  • Required Position Size: $1,000

By calculating your size based on your risk, you ensure that no single market event can wipe out your hard-earned capital.

title: ATR Stops (14)
description: Volatility-adjusted stops using Average True Range to avoid noise-driven exits.
content:
Why ATR Stops Matter

Fixed-distance stops ignore regime changes. In quiet regimes, they are too wide (wasting RR); in volatile regimes, they are too tight (death by a thousand cuts). ATR (Average True Range) measures recent volatility, letting your stop 'breathe' when the market expands and tighten when it compresses.

Core Definitions

  • ATR(14): 14-period average of true range (max of high-low, |high-prevClose|, |low-prevClose|).
  • Stop Multiplier k: Volatility buffer factor; typical k ∈ [1.0, 2.5].
  • Structure Stop: A stop placed beyond a swing high/low or key level.

Formulas

Long stop = Entry - k × ATR(14) • Short stop = Entry + k × ATR(14)

Choose k by regime: trending and noisy → higher k; tight ranges → lower k. Always compare to a structure stop and use the more protective of the two.

Position Sizing with ATR

  1. Define account risk per trade, e.g. 1.5% of equity.
  2. Compute stop distance: d = k × ATR (or use structure if larger).
  3. Position size: size = (risk amount) ÷ d.

Example: Equity $2,000; 1.5% risk ⇒ $30. ATR=0.05; k=1.6 ⇒ d=$0.08. Size ≈ $30 / $0.08 = 375 units.
